Employee.title: Employee
Employee.description: Represents an employee in the system, holding their personal and work-related details.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Employee entity.

firstName.description: The first name of the employee.

lastName.description: The last name of the employee.

employeeIdNumber.description: A unique identifier assigned to the employee by the company.

email.description: The professional email address of the employee.

department.description: The department to which the employee belongs.

dateHired.description: The date when the employee was hired.

AttendanceRecord.title: AttendanceRecord
AttendanceRecord.description: Records an employee's clock-in and clock-out times for a specific period.

id.description: Unique identifier for the AttendanceRecord entity.

employeeId.description: Reference to the Employee who made this attendance record. (Relationship: Employee 1:N AttendanceRecord)

clockInDateTime.description: The date and time when the employee clocked in.

clockOutDateTime.description: The date and time when the employee clocked out. This can be null if the employee has not yet clocked out.

notes.description: Optional notes or remarks for this attendance record.

definition.description: Stores individual employee profiles. The document ID (`employeeFirebaseUid`) is the Firebase User ID of the employee, linking the profile directly to their authentication identity. Includes denormalized 'id' field matching 'employeeFirebaseUid' for consistency.

params.name: employeeFirebaseUid
params.description: The Firebase User ID (UID) of the employee, which serves as the document ID for their employee profile.

definition.description: Stores attendance records (clock-in/clock-out) for a specific employee. Nested under the owning employee's profile for clear ownership and simplified authorization. The 'employeeId' field within the document must match the 'employeeFirebaseUid' from the path for authorization independence.

params.name: employeeFirebaseUid
params.description: The Firebase User ID (UID) of the employee to whom these attendance records belong.

params.name: attendanceRecordId
params.description: The unique identifier for an individual attendance record.

definition.description: A top-level collection used for Database-Backed Access Control (DBAC). The existence of a document at this path signifies that the `adminFirebaseUid` is an administrator. This enables authorization independence without using custom claims.

params.name: adminFirebaseUid
params.description: The Firebase User ID (UID) of a user who has been granted administrative privileges.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to be secure, scalable, and debuggable by strictly adhering to the core design principles and strategy mandates. 

**Authorization Independence via Denormalization:**
1.  **Employee Records (`/employees/{employeeFirebaseUid}`):** Each employee's main profile document is stored with their Firebase User ID (`request.auth.uid`) as the document ID. This direct mapping makes ownership explicit and avoids any `get()` calls to verify ownership. An employee can modify their own record if `request.auth.uid` matches the document ID in the path. Administrative access is managed through a separate, top-level `roles_admin` collection.
2.  **Attendance Records (`/employees/{employeeFirebaseUid}/attendanceRecords/{attendanceRecordId}`):** Attendance records are nested directly under the corresponding employee's document. The `employeeId` field within the `AttendanceRecord` document will explicitly store the `employeeFirebaseUid` from its parent path. This ensures that the ownership context is always available directly within the path and the document itself, eliminating any need for `get()` calls to a parent `Employee` document to check who owns the attendance record. Rules can directly compare `request.auth.uid` with `employeeFirebaseUid` from the path.
3.  **Admin Roles (`/roles_admin/{adminFirebaseUid}`):** A dedicated, top-level collection holds documents where the ID is the Firebase User ID of an administrator. The mere *existence* of a document at `roles_admin/$(request.auth.uid)` grants administrative privileges. This method is fully authorization independent as it requires no complex lookups or hierarchical dependencies.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters) and Structural Segregation:**
1.  **Employee Management:** The `/employees` collection is designed with a homogeneous security posture. Only the owning `employeeFirebaseUid` or a verified administrator can `get`, `update`, or `delete` an employee record. An employee can `get` their own record (`allow get: if request.auth.uid == employeeFirebaseUid;`). Administrators can `list` and `get` all employee records (`allow list, get: if exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/roles_admin/$(request.auth.uid));`). This prevents unauthorized `list` operations while allowing legitimate ones based on explicit permissions.
2.  **Attendance Input and Reporting:** The nested `/employees/{employeeFirebaseUid}/attendanceRecords` subcollection ensures that `list` operations are naturally scoped to the owning employee. An employee can `list` all their own attendance records by querying `employees/$(request.auth.uid)/attendanceRecords`. This is QAP-compliant because the path itself filters the data based on the authenticated user's ID. Administrators, by virtue of their role, can `list` attendance records for any specific employee's subcollection or use collection group queries (with appropriate indexes and rules) to view all attendance records across the application, without relying on client-side filtering or inefficient security rules.

This structure prioritizes clear ownership, explicit roles, and direct path-based authorization, making security rules straightforward to write, test, and debug, while supporting efficient query patterns.
